title: AI in School: Handle With Care

modules.title: The Essay the AI Wrote for Everyone
modules.sub: Unpacking what happens when AI writes for students
modules.description: Learners follow a classroom scenario where students use AI to draft essays and must wrestle with who actually did the learning. They examine what authorship means when AI is involved and begin forming their own position on where the line should be drawn.

modules.title: Surveillance, Grades, and Predictive Systems
modules.sub: Exploring how schools use AI to watch and predict
modules.description: Learners investigate how AI tools can monitor student behaviour, flag attendance patterns, and predict academic outcomes — often without students knowing. They weigh the benefits of early intervention against the risks of being profiled and judged by an algorithm.

modules.title: When Personalization Becomes a Box
modules.sub: Questioning whether tailored learning limits or liberates
modules.description: Learners explore how AI-driven personalisation can quietly steer students toward certain content, difficulty levels, or career paths based on past data. They analyse real scenarios where well-meaning adaptation may reinforce inequality or narrow a student's sense of what they can achieve.

modules.title: Academic Integrity in the AI Era
modules.sub: Defining fairness and honesty when AI blurs the rules
modules.description: Learners examine how traditional notions of cheating no longer map cleanly onto AI-assisted work, and how schools are struggling to write rules that keep up. They debate real cases and draft their own definitions of what honest, meaningful work should look like in an AI-enabled classroom.

modules.title: What Schools Won't Always Tell You
modules.sub: Uncovering data rights and transparency gaps in schools
modules.description: Learners dig into what schools may not disclose about the AI tools they deploy — including who owns student data, how long it is kept, and what it might be used for. They practise reading privacy policies critically and identify the questions every student has a right to ask.

modules.title: Propose Your School's AI Use Policy
modules.sub: Building a real, evidence-based AI policy for your school
modules.description: Learners synthesise everything from previous modules to draft a concrete AI use policy they could genuinely present to a teacher, principal, or school board. They must balance competing interests — learning, fairness, privacy, and practicality — and justify every recommendation with evidence from the course.

synopsis: Follow three students navigating a school that has rolled out AI tools across every subject â and discover the tensions between convenience, fairness, privacy, and learning that nobody fully thought through. This course challenges you to think like a policy-maker and produce a realistic proposal for how AI should and shouldn't be used in your own school.
rationale: Schools are deploying AI tools â from essay detectors to predictive grading â faster than students are being informed of the risks, rights, and ethical trade-offs involved, creating an urgent need for critical, applied understanding at the secondary level.
learning_outcome: Students can critically analyze the risks of specific AI applications in educational settings â including surveillance, bias, and academic integrity â and produce a reasoned, evidence-based policy recommendation they could present to real stakeholders.
source_signals: The Risks of AI in Schools Outweigh the Benefits, Report Says
AI Teacher Assistants Are Useful but Can Pose Risks in Classroom, Report Finds
Teachers Want 'Guardrails and Guidance' on AI Use, Experts Tell Congress
Tali Horowitz: The Key Issue with AI Chatbots and Teens
